summary.goal: Measure context-payload size for representative nodes: memex context vs. git log + PR bodies — supporting evidence for case studies (refs #38)
decisions: Picked three nodes spanning strata: 5a17fac4 (decision-heavy, 4 decisions / 3 rejected), b95077e3 (refactor with motivation, 1 decision / 1 rejected), 27ed3dfa (shallow MSRV, 2 decisions / 0 rejected). Selected by ranking nodes by rejected_approaches count and inspecting the top candidates rather than picking by recency or familiarity.

Byte and char counts over a real tokenizer (tiktoken/anthropic-tokenizer): no Python/API dep, deterministic, the ratio between sources is what matters for an order-of-magnitude comparison; chars/4 is the standard rough approximation.
Reported the shallow-node case (27ed3dfa) where memex is ~102% of the baseline. The negative control is more credible than the wins it sits next to.

Three context sources (memex / git log on key_artifacts / gh pr body) instead of #38's three arms (memex / git-history / transcript-dump). Drops transcript-dump (Claude Code does not persist sessions; reconstructing one authors a strawman) and replaces 'git-history' with the two concrete commands a real user actually runs.

rejected_approaches.description: Run issue #38 as written: 12-15 tasks, 3 arms, N=5 trials, 2 models, LLM judge, Wilcoxon signed-rank
rejected_approaches.reason: Structural selection bias (corpus drawn from memex-using repos), tautological rejected-approach scoring (only arm C has them as input), baseline contamination (commit messages were authored by memex users), arm B may not exist (Claude Code does not persist transcripts), several-hundred-dollar API spend before the rig is even validated. Token count is also a proxy for the wrong thing — what memex improves is workflow friction, not token economy.

rejected_approaches.description: Compare only memex context vs. PR body, omitting git log on key_artifacts
rejected_approaches.reason: git log on the affected files is what a thoughtful non-memex user would actually paste; omitting it would be a strawman baseline. Including it makes the comparison harder for memex but more credible.

rejected_approaches.description: Limit git log to a time window (e.g. last 90 days before node creation)
rejected_approaches.reason: Arbitrary cutoff that would shrink the baseline. Full git log on the artifact files is the honest "everything available to the user" upper bound; users who scope tighter can read that as ceiling. The ratio that matters is order-of-magnitude, not exact.

open_threads: If adoption is later blocked by credible 'show me a number' skepticism, revisit a slimmed benchmark: one repo, N=3, memex vs git-log only (drop transcript-dump), use 'diff compiles + tests pass' as quality signal instead of an LLM judge. Estimated cost ~5-10% of issue #38.
Phase 2 case studies in README/docs need to quote these numbers as footnotes; the persuasive surface is the side-by-side excerpts, not the table here. Phase 2 is a separate downstream node.

Should context-size.sh accept --depth N to show how memex's payload scales with depth? Deferred — current depth=3 matches what AGENTS.md tells users to run, and adding the flag now would be premature.
